title: Känguru der Mathematik 2021 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2021_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), read and verified.

questions.question_text: A kangaroo laid out 3 sticks of equal length to make a shape, without breaking or bending them. Which shape could the kangaroo make?
questions.solution: Each straight stick can only make one straight line. Three sticks crossing at a single centre make a 6-pointed star (3 lines = 6 rays). That is option E. The other shapes need 4 or more sticks or bent sticks.
solution_steps: One straight stick = one straight line.
3 sticks crossing at the centre make 3 lines = a 6-pointed star.
Only option E uses exactly 3 straight, unbent sticks.

questions.question_text: The picture shows 2 mushrooms. What is the difference between their heights?
questions.solution: Read each mushroom's height off the scale. The tall one reaches 12 and the short one reaches 7, so the difference is 12 − 7 = 5.
solution_steps: Read the tall mushroom's height: 12.
Read the short mushroom's height: 7.
Difference = 12 − 7 = 5.

questions.question_text: Which of the paths shown in the pictures is the longest?
questions.solution: Count the grid segments that make up each path. Path A is made of the most unit segments, so it is the longest.
solution_steps: Count the little grid edges in each path.
Compare the totals.
Path A has the most segments, so it is the longest.

questions.question_text: Four identical pieces of paper are placed as shown. Michael wants to punch a hole that goes through all four pieces. At which point should Michael punch the hole?
questions.solution: The hole must lie on the part where all four sheets overlap. Only point D sits inside every one of the four pieces.
solution_steps: Find the region covered by all four sheets at once.
Check which labelled point lies in that shared region.
Only point D is on all four pieces.

questions.question_text: Ella puts on this t-shirt with '2021' and stands in front of a mirror. Which of these images does she see in the mirror?
questions.solution: A mirror flips the picture left-to-right. The number 2021 reversed and flipped looks like option A.
solution_steps: A mirror reflects left and right (a horizontal flip).
Flip '2021' left-to-right, flipping each digit too.
The result is option A.

questions.question_text: The pink tower is taller than the red tower but shorter than the green tower. The silver tower is taller than the green tower. Which tower is the tallest?
questions.solution: Order them from the clues: red < pink < green < silver. The silver tower is the tallest.
solution_steps: red < pink (pink taller than red).
pink < green, and green < silver.
So red < pink < green < silver — silver is tallest.

questions.question_text: These children are standing in a line. Some are facing forwards and others are facing backwards. How many children are holding another child's hand with their right hand?
questions.solution: For each child, find which side is the right hand (it depends on whether they face forward or backward) and check if that hand holds a neighbour. Counting along the line, 6 children hold a neighbour's hand with their right hand.
solution_steps: Decide each child's right hand from the way they face.
Check whether that right hand is holding a neighbour.
Counting all of them gives 6.

questions.question_text: In the Kangaroo constellation, all stars have a number greater than 3 and their sum is 20. Which is the Kangaroo constellation?
questions.solution: Two rules must both hold: every star number is greater than 3, and all the numbers add up to 20. Only constellation B has all stars above 3 with a total of exactly 20.
solution_steps: Reject any constellation that has a star numbered 3 or less.
Among the rest, add the star numbers.
Only constellation B has all stars > 3 and a sum of 20.

questions.question_text: Edmund cut a ribbon as shown in the picture (folded in a zig-zag, then cut along the dashed line). How many pieces of the ribbon did he finish with?
questions.solution: The single cut crosses every layer of the folded ribbon. Counting how many times the dashed line passes through the ribbon and adding the end pieces gives 12 pieces.
solution_steps: The straight cut goes through every folded layer.
Each place the cut crosses the ribbon makes a new break.
Counting all the breaks gives 12 pieces.

questions.question_text: Rose the cat walks along the wall. She starts at point B and follows the direction of the arrows. The cat walks a total of 20 metres. Where does she end up?
questions.solution: The five walls measure 4 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 3 = 15 metres all the way round. After one full loop (15 m) Rose is back at B with 5 m left: B→C is 4 m (now 4) and C→D is 1 m (now 5). She ends at D.
solution_steps: One full loop around the walls = 4+1+5+2+3 = 15 m, ending back at B.
20 − 15 = 5 m still to walk from B.
B→C (4 m) then C→D (1 m) uses the 5 m, so she ends at D.

questions.question_text: Julie and Angela played 'kangball'. Each goal scores 2 points. Julie scored 5 goals and Angela scored 9 goals. How many more points than Julie did Angela score?
questions.solution: Angela scored 9 − 5 = 4 more goals than Julie, and each goal is worth 2 points, so that is 4 × 2 = 8 more points.
solution_steps: Angela's extra goals: 9 − 5 = 4.
Each goal is worth 2 points.
4 × 2 = 8 more points.

questions.question_text: Julia has two pots with flowers. She keeps the flowers where they are and buys more so that, afterwards, each pot has the same number of each type of flower. What is the smallest number of flowers she needs to buy?
questions.solution: For each type of flower, both pots must end with the same count — the larger of the two amounts. Adding the missing flowers to bring each pot up to that amount for both types requires buying 8 flowers in total.
solution_steps: For each flower type, both pots must reach the larger of the two counts.
Add the flowers each pot is missing for both types.
The missing flowers add up to 8.

questions.question_text: Tom encodes words using the board shown. For example, PIZZA has the code A2 A4 C1 C1 B2. What word did Tom encode as B3 B2 C4 D2?
questions.solution: Each code is column-letter + row-number. B3 = column B, row 3 = M. B2 = A. C4 = T. D2 = H. The word is MATH.
solution_steps: Read each code as column then row on the board.
B3 = M, B2 = A, C4 = T, D2 = H.
Putting them together spells MATH.

questions.question_text: Which figure can be made from the 2 pieces shown on the right?
questions.solution: Try fitting the two given pieces together without rotating colours wrongly. Only figure A can be formed exactly from the two pieces.
solution_steps: Look at the two coloured pieces you are given.
Try to fit them together to match each figure.
Only figure A is built exactly from the two pieces.

questions.question_text: The picture shows the five houses of five friends and their school. To go to school, Doris and Ali walk past Leo's house. Eva walks past Chole's house. Which is Eva's house?
questions.solution: Follow the roads to the school. The house whose path passes Chole's house, matching Eva's clue, is house B.
solution_steps: Trace each friend's road to the school.
Doris and Ali's roads pass Leo's house; that fixes Leo.
Eva's road passes Chole's house — that is house B.

questions.question_text: The kangaroo had two branches, each with 10 leaves. It ate some leaves from one branch. Then, from the second branch, it ate as many leaves as were left on the first branch. How many leaves in total were left on the two branches?
questions.solution: Say the kangaroo left L leaves on the first branch (so it ate 10 − L there). From the second branch it ate L leaves, leaving 10 − L. Total left = L + (10 − L) = 10, no matter what L is.
solution_steps: Let L leaves stay on the first branch.
From the second branch it eats L, leaving 10 − L.
Total left = L + (10 − L) = 10.

questions.question_text: Mara built the square by using 4 of the following 5 shapes. Which shape was NOT used?
questions.solution: Match the symbols (stars, diamonds, circles, arrows) in the finished 4x4 square against each piece. The pieces that fit cover the whole square using four of them; the leftover piece is D.
solution_steps: Look at where the stars, diamonds, circles and arrows sit in the square.
Fit four of the pieces into the square with no overlaps.
The piece left over and not used is D.

questions.question_text: Every time the witch has 3 apples she turns them into 1 banana. Every time she has 3 bananas she turns them into 1 apple. What will she finish with if she starts with 4 apples and 5 bananas?
questions.solution: Start 4 apples, 5 bananas. Turn 3 apples into 1 banana → 1 apple, 6 bananas. Turn 6 bananas into 2 apples → 3 apples, 0 bananas. Turn 3 apples into 1 banana → 0 apples, 1 banana. She finishes with one banana.
solution_steps: 4 apples, 5 bananas → (3 apples→1 banana) → 1 apple, 6 bananas.
6 bananas → 2 apples → 3 apples, 0 bananas.
3 apples → 1 banana → finishes with just 1 banana.

questions.question_text: The cards 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 are placed into 2 boxes so the sums in each box are equal. Which number must be in the box with the number 4?
questions.solution: All five cards total 2+3+4+5+6 = 20, so each box must sum to 10. The box with 4 needs 6 more from the other cards, and the only way to make 6 is the single card 6. So 6 must share the box with 4.
solution_steps: Total of all cards = 20, so each box sums to 10.
The box with 4 needs 10 − 4 = 6 more.
Only the card 6 makes 6, so 6 is with 4.

questions.question_text: The picture shows two cogs, each with a black tooth. Where will the black teeth be after the small cog has made one full turn?
questions.solution: After one full turn the small cog's black tooth is back where it started. The big cog has more teeth, so it turns only part of the way round — by the number of teeth the small cog has. Tracking that partial turn lands the black teeth as shown in option C.
solution_steps: One full turn brings the small cog's black tooth back to the start.
The bigger cog turns less — only as far as the small cog's tooth count.
That partial turn of the big cog matches option C.

questions.question_text: 3 girls and 2 boys were dancing. They danced in pairs so that each girl danced with each boy for exactly 1 minute. At any time there was only one pair on the floor. For how many minutes did they dance?
questions.solution: Each of the 3 girls dances with each of the 2 boys: 3 × 2 = 6 pairs. Only one pair dances at a time for 1 minute each, so the dancing lasts 6 minutes.
solution_steps: Number of girl-boy pairs = 3 × 2 = 6.
Each pair dances 1 minute, one at a time.
Total = 6 minutes.

questions.question_text: Each participant in a cooking contest baked one tray of cookies like the one shown beside. What is the smallest number of trays of cookies needed to make the plate shown?
questions.solution: For each kind of cookie, work out how many trays are needed so there are enough for the plate. The cookie type that runs short the fastest needs 3 trays, so 3 trays are required.
solution_steps: Count how many of each cookie the plate needs.
Count how many of each cookie one tray provides.
The type needing the most trays needs 3, so 3 trays in all.

questions.question_text: Kangie eats only apples on Monday, Wednesday and Friday. On Tuesdays and Thursdays he eats only mangoes. He eats either 2 apples or 3 mangoes a day. On Saturdays and Sundays he eats nothing. How many pieces of fruit does Kangie eat in two weeks?
questions.solution: Each week: 3 apple-days × 2 apples = 6 apples, plus 2 mango-days × 3 mangoes = 6 mangoes = 12 pieces. Two weeks = 2 × 12 = 24 pieces.
solution_steps: Apple days (Mon, Wed, Fri): 3 × 2 = 6 apples a week.
Mango days (Tue, Thu): 2 × 3 = 6 mangoes a week → 12 per week.
Two weeks: 2 × 12 = 24 pieces.

questions.question_text: Stan has five toys: a ball, blocks, a game, a puzzle and a car, each on a different shelf. The ball is higher than the blocks and lower than the car. The game is directly above the ball. On which shelf can the puzzle NOT be placed?
questions.solution: The ball must have blocks below it, the game just above it (game = ball + 1), and the car higher still. Checking the valid arrangements, shelf 3 is always taken by the ball or the game, never the puzzle. So the puzzle cannot go on shelf 3.
solution_steps: Order rules: blocks < ball < car, and game = ball + 1 (right above).
This forces the ball to shelf 2 or 3 and the game right above it.
In every valid setup shelf 3 holds the ball or the game, so the puzzle can never be on shelf 3.
